coredata.dc:title: Construction and properties of a carbon dots-decorated gelatin-dialdehyde starch hydrogel with pH response release and antibacterial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Tianqi
Wu, Yue
Wang, Zhaohua
Ban, Qingfeng
Cheng, Jianjun
coredata.dc:description: An antibacterial carbon dot hydrogel (GDSS-PCD) was constructed based on gelatin, dialdehyde starch (DS) and carbon dots (S-PCDs). The formation mechanism of GDSS-PCD hydrogels was attributed to the synergistic cross-linking of hydrogen bonds and dynamic covalent bonds. With increasing S-PCD content, the mechanical and rheological properties of GDSS-PCD hydrogels can be improved, and the micropore size becomes denser. GDSS-PCD hydrogels had pH-dependent swelling and degradation behavior, with a high swelling rate under acidic conditions and relatively low swelling under neutral and alkaline conditions. The cumulative release of S-PCDs from the same hydrogel in an acidic environment was higher than that in an alkaline environment, indicating that the GDSS-PCD hydrogel had a pH-dependent controlled release ability. The release behavior of S-PCDs conformed to the first-order kinetic release model (R2 > 0.95), and the release mechanism was related to Fickian diffusion. The synergistic antibacterial mechanism of GDSS-PCD hydrogels against Staphylococcus aureus suggested that bacterial metabolism leads to an acidic culture environment, which releases S-PCDs and destroys the bacterial cell membrane for antibacterial purposes. In GDSS-PCD hydrogels, S-PCDs play the main antibacterial role, and the hydrogel plays a synergistic role in trapping bacteria. Carbon dot hydrogels are promising materials to fulfil the functions of antibacterial and controlled release in the food and biomedical fields.

head.ce:title: Construction and properties of a carbon dots-decorated gelatin-dialdehyde starch hydrogel with pH response release and antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, 150030, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: No. 600, Changjiang Road, Harbin 150000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An antibacterial carbon dot hydrogel (GDSS-PCD) was constructed based on gelatin, dialdehyde starch (DS) and carbon dots (S-PCDs). The formation mechanism of GDSS-PCD hydrogels was attributed to the synergistic cross-linking of hydrogen bonds and dynamic covalent bonds. With increasing S-PCD content, the mechanical and rheological properties of GDSS-PCD hydrogels can be improved, and the micropore size becomes denser. GDSS-PCD hydrogels had pH-dependent swelling and degradation behavior, with a high swelling rate under acidic conditions and relatively low swelling under neutral and alkaline conditions. The cumulative release of S-PCDs from the same hydrogel in an acidic environment was higher than that in an alkaline environment, indicating that the GDSS-PCD hydrogel had a pH-dependent controlled release ability. The release behavior of S-PCDs conformed to the first-order kinetic release model (R2 > 0.95), and the release mechanism was related to Fickian diffusion. The synergistic antibacterial mechanism of GDSS-PCD hydrogels against Staphylococcus aureus suggested that bacterial metabolism leads to an acidic culture environment, which releases S-PCDs and destroys the bacterial cell membrane for antibacterial purposes. In GDSS-PCD hydrogels, S-PCDs play the main antibacterial role, and the hydrogel plays a synergistic role in trapping bacteria. Carbon dot hydrogels are promising materials to fulfil the functions of antibacterial and controlled release in the food and biomedical fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections are daunting and pose a serious threat to modern health care. Antibiotics, chemical antimicrobials, and other microbial fungicides are effective strategies against foodborne pathogens. However, widespread use of antibiotics has led to the rapid progression and spread of antimicrobial resistance (AMR), which poses a greater problem to global public health [1]. Therefore, killing foodborne pathogens is necessary, which necessitates the development of antibacterial materials with high biosafety and can prevent AMR progression.

Hydrogels are materials with high water content and a three-dimensional porous structure. Hydrogels prepared by physical and chemical synergistic crosslinking of natural polymers have excellent biocompatibility and mechanical properties, and have a wide range of application prospects [2]. Various natural polymers have certain advantages as carriers of bioactive substances due to their biodegradability, biocompatibility, inherent bioactivity and safety [3]. In addition, hydrogels with antibacterial properties have been used in the biomedical and food fields as wound healing excipients or smart food packaging films [4,5]. In general, conventional hydrogels can be antibacterial in two ways. One is that the precursors to prepare hydrogels have antibacterial properties to achieve contact antibacterial effects [6,7]. The other is to load antibacterial substances into the hydrogel network to achieve antibacterial effects by releasing them [8].

Among many antibacterial materials, carbon dots (CDs), which are surface functionalized carbon-based nanoparticles with a diameter of <10 nm, have received extensive attention due to their excellent fluorescence properties, good water solubility and biocompatibility, and structural controllability [9,10]. CDs can not only show good antibacterial activity, but also effectively destroy bacterial biofilms [11]. Moreover, CDs have multiple antibacterial mechanisms, which are conducive to avoiding the emergence of bacterial resistance, and have good antibacterial prospects [5]. Loading CDs into the hydrogel network to construct hydrogels with antibacterial properties has been frequently reported. Yang et al. and Liu et al. synthesized carbon dots enriched with −NH2 groups on the surface, and prepared carbon dot hydrogels with good antibacterial activity by linking oxidized dextran with Schiff-base [4,5]. Cui et al. fixed antibacterial cationic CDs into the three-dimensional network of hydrogels and released them in response to environmental changes caused by bacterial growth, thereby exerting antibacterial activity [12].

Our previous work produced injectable hydrogels with biocompatible, tunable mechanical properties and self-healing properties using gelatin and dialdehyde starch as raw materials [13]. In addition, spermidine-capped carbon dots (S-PCDs) containing a large number of amino groups on the surface were synthesized by a hydrothermal reaction. In this study, antibacterial carbon dots were introduced into a well-performing gelatin and dialdehyde starch hydrogel system to construct carbon dot-released antibacterial hydrogels. The crosslinking mechanism, microstructure and rheological properties of hydrogels were characterized. The swelling and degradation behavior of the carbon dots hydrogels in response to pH were investigated, and the release behavior and mechanism of the carbon dots were analyzed. This study provides a good reference for the antibacterial application of CD-loaded hydrogels. The prepared hydrogel can be used as an environmentally sensitive antiseptic in acidic packaging environments caused by bacteria, and can also provide an effective antibacterial biomaterial for wound healing.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI10000, analytical reagent) was provided by Shanghai Macrlin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Spermidine (99 %) and kanamycin were purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Dialdehyde starch (DS, aldehyde degree: 95 %) was purchased from Jinshan Modified Starch Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Gelatin (G, 100 g Bloom) was provided by Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

S-PCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis according to Cui et al. [12]. The synthetic route is shown in Fig. 1a. PEI carbon dots (P-CDs) were synthesized by adding PEI at a concentration of 10 mg/mL into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined hydrothermal reaction autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 2 h. Then, spermidine-capped carbon dots (S-PCDs) were synthesized by adding the same mass of spermidine to the cooled P-CD solution and heated at 220 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the yellowish suspension of S-PCDs was collected, centrifuged at 10500 ×g for 30 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. The supernatant was dialyzed in a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag with deionized water for 5 h, and the dialysate was changed every 1 h. The prepared S-PCDs were stored at 4 °C or freeze-dried in vacuum for use.

The carbon dot hydrogels were formed by a one-pot method according to Bhattacharya et al. [14]. The synthetic route is shown in Fig. 1a, b. Gelatin (10 g) was added to 100 mL of deionized water and slowly stirred at 65 °C for 20 min until completely dissolved, and then the clear solution was placed at 40 °C for use. Dialdehyde starch (DS, 10 g/100 mL) was stirred in a boiling water bath until it became a paste, and then the pH was adjusted to approximately 10 with 2 mol/L NaOH. The S-PCDs were dissolved in gelatin solution and stirred to form a uniform solution, then the DS solution was added and transferred to a mold and sealed overnight to form carbon dot hydrogels. The hydrogels without S-PCDs were defined as GDS hydrogel, and the hydrogels formed after the addition of 10–40 mg of S-PCDs powder (1–4 mg/mL carbon dots, depending on the volume of gelatin solution) were defined as GDSS-PCD hydrogels, including GDSS-PCD1, GDSS-PCD2, GDSS-PCD3 and GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels. Supplementary Table 1 details the raw material ratios of various carbon dot hydrogels.

The microstructure of the GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels was observed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-3400 N, Japan) at 5.0 kV. Hydrogels with a diameter of 12 mm and length of 100 mm were frozen with liquid nitrogen and then vacuum freeze-dried for 48 h. The dried hydrogels were cut along the cross section and placed on the SEM platform to be sputtered with gold. The microstructure was observed at 500 and 5000× magnifications. At least 100 data points were collected to calculate and analyze the pore size of the hydrogels using the image analysis software Nano Measurer System 1.2 [15].

After vacuum freeze-drying for 48 h, the GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels were ground to obtain hydrogel powder. The chemical bond changes of gelatin, DS, GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels were characterized by an FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA). FT-IR spectra were recorded in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 against a background of ambient air with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and scanned 32 times, three times for each hydrogel [16].

Texture property analysis (TPA) was measured by a texture analyzer (TA-XT plus C, Stable Micro Systems, UK). Cylindrical GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels (diameter 12 mm, height 10 mm) were placed on the loading platform, and a P75 probe was used for the TPA test. The pretest speed was 2.0 mm/s, the test speed was 2.0 mm/s, the posttest speed was 2.0 mm/s, the compression distance was 5 mm, and the trigger force was 5.0 g [17].

A rotating rheometer (Haake Mars 40, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA) equipped with a 35 mm circular plate probe was selected to measure the rheological behavior of hydrogels, and the parallel plate spacing was set to 1 mm.

The GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels (35 mm in diameter, 1–2 mm in thickness) were placed on the lower plate. The fixed angular frequency was 1 Hz. The storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G") of the hydrogels were determined by amplitude strain scanning (γ = 0.1–100 %), and the linear viscoelastic region (LVR) of the hydrogels was obtained. In addition, a frequency scanning experiment (f = 0.1–10 Hz) was performed at 25 °C with the strain fixed at 10 %, a plate fixture diameter of 35 mm, and a test gap of 1 mm.

Alternate step strain tests were conducted according to the methods of Wei et al. [18] and Cui et al. [13]. First, the prepared GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels (diameter of 35 mm, thickness of 1–2 mm) were placed on the lower plate with a fixed angular frequency of 1 Hz. Amplitude strain scanning (γ = 0.1–1000 %) was performed to determine the intersection of G' and G" of the hydrogels. The critical point of hydrogel transition from gel to sol was determined. Next, after determining the intersection point of G' and G" of hydrogels, two strains less than and greater than the intersection point strain were determined. Alternate step strain scanning was carried out at a fixed angular frequency (1 Hz), and the changes in G' and G" during the strain changes were measured using time as the horizontal coordinate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling and degradation properties of hydrogels

ce:section.ce:para:
The swelling properties of GDSS-PCD hydrogels at 37 °C were characterized by the increase in hydrogel mass with time. The initial weight of the cylindrical hydrogels (12 mm diameter, 5 mm height) was weighed and recorded. The hydrogels were then placed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffers of different acidity and alkalinity (pH 3, 5, 7, and 9) to observe the change in hydrogel mass. The weight of the hydrogels was recorded at intervals until swelling equilibrium was reached and the weight remained constant. During swelling measurements, each time the hydrogels were removed, the surface of the hydrogels was wiped with a clean filter paper to absorb excess water and weighed. Each set was repeated at least four times. The swelling rate of the hydrogel was calculated [19]:
(1)
Swelling rate =
Mt – M 0
M 0
× 100 %
where Mt and M0 are the weight of the hydrogel at time t and 0, respectively.

The degradation behavior of hydrogels was evaluated by measuring the mass loss of GDSS-PCD hydrogels. The hydrogels (12 mm diameter, 5 mm height) were weighed (W0) and dipped into centrifuge tubes containing 20 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH 3, 5, 7, and 9). After removing the hydrogels at a fixed time, the surface water was immediately absorbed by filter paper and weighed (Wt). The mass loss of hydrogels was calculated by Li et al. [20]. In addition, after degrading the hydrogels for 6 h, 12 h and 48 h, the hydrogels were frozen in liquid nitrogen and vacuum freeze-dried for 24 h. Then the morphology of the hydrogels was photographed by SEM to observe the changes in the microstructure of the hydrogels.
(2)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release of carbon dots from carbon dot hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the study of Cheng et al. [19], the release kinetics of GDSS-PCD hydrogels were analyzed. The GDSS-PCD hydrogels were weighed and placed in centrifuge tubes containing 50 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH 3, 5, 7, and 9), 3 mL of supernatant was extracted regularly, and 3 mL of buffer solution with the same pH was added to the corresponding centrifuge tube. The extracted supernatant was centrifuged and the fluorescence intensity at 364 nm was determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The concentration of S-PCDs was calculated according to the preestablished standard curve. The cumulative release of S-PCDs was calculated as follows:
(3)
Cumulative release
%
=
Ve
∑
i = 1
n − 1
Ci + V 0 Cn
W 0
× 100 %

where Ve is the replacement volume of PBS (5 mL), V0 is the total volume of PBS (10 mL), Ci and Cn are the release concentrations at different times (mg/mL), and W0 is the number of CDs loaded on the hydrogel (10 mg).

The zero-order model, first-order model, Higuchi model and Korsmeyer-Peppas model were used to analyze the release kinetics of S-PCDs in GDSS-PCD hydrogels [21,22]:
(4)

where Mt is the cumulative release amount at time t, M∞ is the cumulative release amount at time ∞, Mt/Mt is the percentage of cumulative release at time t, k is the kinetic constant, n is the index of the release mechanism, n < 0.43 represents Fickian diffusion, 0.43 < n < 0.85 indicates irregular release, and n > 0.85 indicates corrosion release [19]. The best fitting result is the one with the largest correlation coefficient R and the smallest mean square error.

Before determining the antibacterial activity, the hydrogels were pretreated as follows [12]. First, all hydrogels (12 mm in diameter and 2 mm in thickness) were cleaned several times with deionized water and 75 % ethanol solution to remove uncrosslinked monomers and impurities. In addition, the hydrogels were sterilized by soaking in 75 % ethanol solution for 2 h and then transferred to a sterile workbench. The hydrogels were repeatedly rinsed in sterile PBS several times to remove the ethanol solution and then measured to ensure that the pH of the soaking solution was neutral. Finally, the hydrogels were transferred to 24-well cell culture plates for use.

Staphylococcus aureus was cultured in LB broth. For the determination of antibacterial activity, GDSS-PCD hydrogels were used as the experimental group, and GDS hydrogel and S-PCDs were used as the control group. Three parallel experiments were performed in each group. The antibacterial activity of hydrogels against S. aureus was measured using the spread plate method. Briefly, bacterial suspensions were cocultured with GDSS-PCD hydrogels, GDS hydrogel and S-PCDs at 37 °C for 24 h. One hundred microlitres of diluted coculture was uniformly spread on LB agar medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The bactericidal activity was calculated by the units of colony formation on the count plate [6,7]. In addition, after treatment with GDSS-PCD hydrogels, GDS hydrogel and S-PCDs, the treated bacteria were fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution for 24 h, dehydrated in a series of ethanol solutions (0, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 %) and freeze-dried. After adding gold onto the sample surface, the morphology of the adherent bacteria was observed using SEM (Hitachi S-3400 N, Japan).

The adhesion ability of bacteria to GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD3 hydrogel was evaluated by incubating S. aureus suspensions (1 × 108 CFU/mL) with hydrogels [23]. Briefly, the GDSS-PCD3 hydrogel and GDS hydrogel were immersed in 500 μL of bacterial suspension and placed at 37 °C for 12 h. After bacterial attachment, the surface of the hydrogels was cleaned with sterile water to remove unattached bacteria, and the attached bacteria were fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution for 24 h. The attached bacteria were then dehydrated in a series of ethanol solutions (0, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 %) and freeze-dried. After sputtering gold onto the sample surface, the morphology of the adherent bacteria was observed using a SEM (Hitachi S-3400 N, Japan).

The experimental results are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (all experiments were repeated at least three times). A significance level of 0.05 was used. Graphs were constructed using Origin Pro 2020 (Northampton, USA). Statistical analyses were performed using IBM SPSS 19.0 (SPSS Inc., USA) software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the carbon dot hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Among GDSS-PCD hydrogels, dialdehyde starch can be used as a natural macromolecular cross-linker for crosslinking gelatin chains. The construction principle of GDSS-PCD hydrogels is based on the large number of active groups on the surface of gelatin, DS and S-PCDs. There are a large number of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of gelatin, hydroxyl and aldehyde groups on the surface of DS, and amino groups on the surface of S-PCDs, respectively, and the active groups give them more cross-linking sites. Reversible dynamic imine bonds can be formed between aldehydes and amino groups by Schiff-base reactions. A large number of hydrogen bonds can be formed between hydroxyl (H) and amino (N), hydroxyl (O) and amino (H), and amino (N) and carboxyl (O) groups (Fig. 1c). S-PCDs can be fixed in the hydrogel network through chemical and hydrogen bonds. Based on the synergistic crosslinking of physics and chemistry, GDSS-PCD hydrogels were constructed. Positively charged antibacterial S-PCDs acted as crosslinkers and bonds in the hydrogel network, and were released in specific environments to play an antibacterial role. In this study, the properties of GDSS-PCD hydrogels with different S-PCD concentrations were investigated using GDS hydrogel as a control. The prepared hydrogels had smooth surfaces with a jelly like appearance and a soft and rough texture (Fig. 1d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure of the carbon dot hydrogel

ce:section.ce:para: The microstructures of the GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels are showen in Fig. 2 . It can be clearly seen that all hydrogels showed interconnected porous structures with pore sizes on the micrometer scale. Fig. 2a1, a2 shows the microstructure and pore size distribution of the GDS hydrogel. The pore size structure of the GDS hydrogel was clear and regular, with an average pore size of 5.29 μm, which was similar to that of other natural polymer hydrogels. The porous structure provides a high specific surface area and sufficient space, which facilitates the capture of more active materials.

After the addition of S-PCDs, a clear three-dimensional network structure can still be observed in the hydrogels, but the pore size distribution and size of the hydrogels were different (Fig. 2b1-f1). After the introduction of S-PCDs into the original GDS hydrogels, the network structure of GDSS-PCD hydrogels became increasingly regular, with significantly smaller pores and smoother connections. The pore sizes of GDSS-PCD1, GDSS-PCD2, GDSS-PCD3, and GDSS-PCD4 were 1.17–10.10 μm, 1.12–6.55 μm, 1.29–5.06 μm, and 1.20–3.12 μm, respectively. When the concentration of S-PCDs was increased from 1 mg/mL to 4 mg/mL, the average pore size of the hydrogel decreased from 4.73 μm to 2.12 μm (Fig. 2c2-f2). This indicated that the introduced S-PCDs can be evenly distributed in the hydrogels and make the network structure of the hydrogels denser by cross-linking with the hydrogels.

The chemical structure and crosslinking mechanism of the GDSS-PCD hydrogels were characterized by FT-IR. Fig. 3a shows the FT-IR spectra of gelatin characteristic bands: The broad absorption band at 3277 cm−1 was attributable to hydroxyl (O − H) groups, which are able to form hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups (−OH) and amino (−NH) groups [24,25]. The characteristic peak at 2971 cm−1 corresponded to the CH2 groups. Amide I and amide II bands of gelatin at 1700–1600 and 1560–1500 cm−1 were reported by Oungbho et al. [26]. The peak at 1627 cm−1 (amide I) was mainly from the CO tensile vibration, 1525 cm−1 (amide II), attributed to a combination of C − N stretching and in-face N − H deformation), and 1230 cm−1 (amide III, corresponding to C − N stretching, N − H in-plane bending and CH2 rocking vibration). The vibration peaks of gelatin at approximately 1100–1000 cm−1 were related to C − C and C − O vibrations. The wide peak at 668 cm−1 may be related to the C − H bond. The peak of DS at 3483 cm−1 was related to the vibration stretching of −OH. The peak at 2932 cm−1 was attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C − H. DS has a characteristic peak at 1727 cm−1, which belongs to the aldehyde group (C=O) stretching vibration [27]. Several characteristic peaks appeared between 1200–1000 cm−1, reflecting the stretching of the C − O bond. S-PCDs had an absorption peak near 3258 cm−1, which was related to the stretching vibration of the amino/amide N − H bond. The characteristic C − H absorption peaks of S-PCDs appeared near 2954 and 2841 cm−1, which are able to form hydrogen bonds with the carbonyl groups attached to peptides in proteins. The deformation vibration peak of N − H was at 1527 cm−1 and there was an obvious characteristic C − H absorption peak at 1444 cm−1, while the absorption peaks were related to the stretching vibration of C − N at 1400–1200 cm−1.

FT-IR diagrams of the GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD hydrogels are shown in Fig. 3b. The broad peak of GDSS-PCD hydrogels at 3278 cm−1 may be related to the formation of −OH and hydrogen bonds. The position of this peak was redshifted compared to the characteristic peaks of gelatin and S-PCDs, and the position of this peak was blueshifted compared to the characteristic peaks of DS, indicating that −OH and −NH2 can form hydrogen bond interactions. Compared with the CO of gelatin at 1627 cm−1 and the hydroxyl group of S-PCDs at 3258 cm−1, the peak position of GDSS-PCD changed, which was related to the hydrogen bonds between CO and −OH [28]. In addition, the characteristic peak at 1629 cm−1 may be related to the CN formed by the Schiff-base reaction. The disappearance of the aldehyde group peak at 1727 cm−1 of DS for the GDSS-PCD hydrogels suggested that the aldehyde group was involved in the Schiff-base reaction. Compared with the GDS hydrogel, the peak intensity of GDSS-PCD hydrogels near 3280 cm−1 was increased significantly, indicating the existence of hydrogen bond interactions between S-PCDs and GDS hydrogel. The peak position of the GDS hydrogel at 1631 cm−1 shifted to that of the GDSS-PCD hydrogels at 1629 cm−1 with a significant increase in intensity, indicating that the addition of S-PCDs can form a Schiff-base reaction with GDS hydrogel. In addition, the peak intensities at 3278 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 increased with the addition of S-PCDs, suggesting that the resulting hydrogen bond and imine bond interactions were enhanced.

Based on the FT-IR results, a possible chemical reaction mechanism was proposed. A large number of dynamic imine bonds can be formed between the amino groups of gelatin and the carboxyl groups of DS by the Schiff-base reaction. The amino groups on the surface of S-PCDs reacted with the carboxyl groups on the surface of gelatin and the hydroxyl groups on the surface of DS to form hydrogen bonds. The amino groups of some S-PCDs will react with the aldehyde groups on the surface of DS to form the Schiff-base, and ultimately form hydrogels with a three-dimensional copolymerization network. This synergistic physical and chemical crosslinking imparts excellent properties to hydrogels, allowing S-PCDs to be fully loaded in the hydrogel network and released in a specific environment [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Texture property analysis of carbon dot hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The texture properties of GDSS-PCD hydrogels are shown in Table 1 . The addition of S-PCDs to the hydrogel system significantly increased the hardness (P < 0.05). As the concentration of S-PCDs increased from 1 mg/mL to 4 mg/mL, the hardness of the hydrogels increased from 496.33 g to 1235.88 g, indicating a more stable hydrogel structure. In addition, the cohesiveness of GDSS-PCD4 increased from 0.57 N to 0.66 N compared with GDSS-PCD1, indicating an increase in the internal bond strength of the final structure of hydrogels and the ability to withstand secondary deformation was enhanced. This result was related to the physicochemical crosslinking formed between S-PCDs and hydrogels. The synergistic effect gave the hydrogel stronger rigidity and a denser network structure. The springiness of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD3 hydrogels was increased to 0.86 mm, 1.02 mm and 1.19 mm, respectively, compared with that of GDS hydrogels. The springiness of the GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels was 1.13 mm, which was higher than that of the GDS hydrogel but lower than that of the GDSS-PCD3 hydrogel. This may be attributed to the higher hardness and cohesiveness of the GDSS-PCD4 hydrogel, resulting in an increase in rigid structure and a relative decrease in toughness. The gumminess and chewiness properties of the hydrogels were enhanced by the addition of 1–4 mg/mL S-PCDs and were positively correlated with the hardness, which indirectly reflected the structural stability of the hydrogels. The results showed that the physical and chemical crosslinking of S-PCDs with the hydrogels improved the hardness and springiness of the hydrogels and made the structure of the hydrogels more stable, denser and stronger. The results of the mechanical properties were consistent with those of the TPA test. Relevant results are attached in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological properties of the carbon dot hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The rheological properties of GDSS-PCD hydrogels were analyzed by frequency scanning, and the results are shown in Fig. 4a. It can be seen from the frequency scanning curve that G' was larger than the corresponding G" throughout the frequency scanning range, indicating that all samples were elastic gel rather than viscous sol. Both G' and G" were almost independent of the frequency range studied, suggesting that the hydrogels can maintain their original shape [29]. The tanδ values of the hydrogels were all <1, indicating that all samples can maintain the ideal elastic state in the applied frequency range. After S-PCDs were introduced into GDS hydrogels, the G' values of GDSS-PCD hydrogels gradually increased with increasing S-PCD concentration. This can be attributed to the fact that the increase in the concentration of S-PCDs leads to the formation of more covalent and hydrogen bonds with hydrogels, thus increasing the crosslinking density. It has been shown that with increasing crosslinking density, the hydrogels had a stronger internal structure and a more stable network structure [30,31].

When hydrogels are subjected to large stress, they will undergo large deformation (exceeding the linear viscoelastic region). For this reason, the alternate step strain scanning method was used to switch from small deformation (G' > G") to large deformation (G'<G"), and multiple measurements were performed to evaluate the recovery performance of the GDSS-PCD hydrogel network. The gel point of hydrogels was determined by the intersection of G' and G" curves obtained by strain scanning. Near this critical point, the state of the hydrogel was between sol and gel. When G'>G", the hydrogels appeared solid-like, with a certain hardness and stability. When G' < G", the internal structure system was weak, and the structure of the hydrogels collapsed (Fig. 4b). The gel points of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels were 457 %, 731 %, 680 % and 1085 % strains, respectively. Strains equal to and higher than this strain were selected for alternate step scanning measurements, as shown in Fig. 4c, d. When the oscillatory shear strain was small, all hydrogels exhibited G' > G", and when the strain increased from a small strain to the critical point strain, G' and G" were close, indicating that the network structure of hydrogels was slightly damaged (Fig. 4c). G' and G" immediately returned to their original values when the strain was reduced to a small strain. When the strain was further increased to a strain above the gel point, G' dropped sharply and was lower than G", indicating that the hydrogel network was broken. However, under low strain conditions, G' quickly recovered to the initial value, indicating that the structure of the hydrogel network can be effectively recovered (Fig. 4d). This repeatable self-recovery behavior can be attributed to dynamic cross-linking based on physical interactions between molecular chains and the Schiff-base reactions in the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling and degradation properties of carbon dot hydrogels

ce:section.ce:para: The swelling behavior of GDSS-PCD hydrogels at different pH values (pH = 3, 5, 7 and 9) is shown in Fig. 5 . GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels showed similar swelling curves, both indicating that the hydrogels rapidly swelled during water absorption within the initial 4 h and gradually reached swelling equilibrium as time progressed. By comparing the swelling rate of the same hydrogel at pH 3, 5, 7 and 9 after 12 h of swelling, it can be seen that the swelling rate of the hydrogels was higher under acidic conditions and relatively lower under alkaline conditions (Fig. 5a), which confirmed the pH responsiveness of the GDSS-PCD hydrogels. After swelling at pH 3, 5, 7 and 9 for 12 h, the swelling rates of the GDSS-PCD1 hydrogel were 180.13 %, 162.37 %, 141.38 and 123.10 %, respectively. The rates of the GDSS-PCD4 hydrogel were 138.65 %, 135.84 %, 120.12 % and 96.13 %, respectively. The swelling degree of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels at low pH values (pH = 3 and 5) was greater than that at neutral pH value (pH = 7). However, as the pH gradually increased to pH = 9, the swelling degree of the hydrogels decreased. At low pH, the hydrogen ions in the hydrogen bonds and the amine groups in the gelatin are protonated, creating a strong electrostatic repulsion force to expand the structure of the hydrogel network allowing interaction with water, which results in a higher swelling rate. As the solution pH increases gradually, the swelling rate decreases due to the weakening of the electrostatic repulsion force after deprotonation [32,33].

In addition to exploring the influence of pH on hydrogels, the influence of different S-PCD concentrations on GDSS-PCD hydrogels was also studied. A comparison of Fig. 5a-d shows that the swelling rate was GDSS-PCD1 > GDSS-PCD2 > GDSS-PCD3 > GDSS-PCD4. After 12 h of swelling, the swelling rates of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels were 180.13 %, 162.32 %, 150.31 % and 138.63 % (at pH = 3), 141.38 %, 125.83 %, 124.36 % and 120.12 % (at pH = 7), and 123.10 %, 106.38 %, 104.99 % and 96.13 % (at pH = 9), respectively. The results showed that the swelling rates of the hydrogel decreased with increasing S-PCD concentration, which was mainly because the higher S-PCD concentration led to a higher crosslinking density to form a denser gel structure, which slowed down the penetration of the medium and further reduced the swelling rate. This result was consistent with the results of the micropore size of hydrogels, where the looser the hydrogel structure, the larger the pore size, the more water absorption, and thus the higher the swelling rate.

With the prolongation of the soaking time, the degradation of hydrogels began to dominate, and the hydrogels gradually lost weight, which was mainly due to structural collapse and decomposition caused by internal crosslinking breaks. The mass ratio of GDSS-PCD hydrogels increased gradually with time from 0 to 24 h, indicating that the hydrogels gradually absorbed water, which was also consistent with the swelling property results. After swelling for 48 h, the mass ratio of all hydrogels showed a decreasing trend compared to 24 h, confirming the degradation behavior of hydrogels. Compared with swelling for 24 h, with a gradual increase in pH from 3 to 9 after 48 h of swelling, the mass ratio of the GDSS-PCD1 hydrogel decreased by 166 %, 145 %, 123 % and 87 %, and the mass ratios of the GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels were 133 %, 128 %, 82 % and 60 %, respectively (Fig. 6a), which suggests that pH also had an impact on the degradation properties of hydrogels. The degradation of hydrogels was more significant under acidic conditions (pH = 3 and 5), while it was relatively slower at pH = 7 and 9.

In addition, at pH 3, the mass ratio of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels decreased from 341 %, 312 %, 300 % and 275 % at 24 h to 175 %, 163 %, 152 % and 172 % at 48 h, respectively (Fig. 6a-d). This indicated that the faster degradation rate of GDSS-PCD1 and the relatively slower degradation of GDSS-PCD4 were related to the increase in S-PCD concentration. The increase in S-PCD concentration leads to an increase in the crosslinking degree of GDSS-PCD hydrogels, and the addition of S-PCDs may delay the degradation rate of the hydrogels due to the existence of multiple crosslinking sites on the surface of the nanoparticles. It has been shown that the concentration of crosslinking agent, the number of degradable groups in hydrogels, and the molecular weight affect the degree of hydrogel degradation [34]. The degradation of the hydrogel was related to the destruction of the hydrogel network structure. The denser the network structure and the greater the degree of crosslinking are, the lower the degradation rate of the hydrogel, while the looser the network structure is, the higher the degradation rate of the hydrogel.

The degradation properties of hydrogels were analyzed by SEM observation (Fig. 6e-g). The GDSS-PCD3 hydrogel had a porous network structure with a uniform and regular pore size distribution and relatively dense connections (Fig. 6e). However, after swelling for 12 h at pH 7, the average pore size became significantly larger, although it still displayed a porous structure (Fig. 6f). After swelling for 48 h, the porous structure of the hydrogel network was destroyed, and there was an obvious large collapse structure, indicating that the structure was gradually collapsing and that the hydrogel was degrading. The swelling stability of pH-sensitive hydrogels corresponded to changes in pH value, and the resulting swelling and degradation behavior enables them to be used in targeted drug delivery systems [32]. Hydrogels have a low swelling rate and a stable and dense structure, which provides effective protection for the loaded active substance. Upon further swelling of the hydrogels, the network structure was disrupted, the hydrogel was gradually degraded, and the active substance loaded was released.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release of S-PCDs in carbon dot hydrogels

ce:section.ce:para: The release process of S-PCDs from hydrogels under different pH conditions was determined by performing release studies at different pH values (3, 5, 7, and 9) (Fig. 7 ). The release process of S-PCDs can be divided into three stages: initial burst, sustained release, and stabilization phases. S-PCDs exist in the form of physical interactions within or on the surface of hydrogels and can be released rapidly in the solution environment. S-PCDs existed in the form of covalent bonds in hydrogels, and the release rate was relatively slow. All hydrogels had a typical release mode under different pH conditions, with explosive release behavior in the initial 60 min, followed by a slow-release stabilization phase. Under the same pH conditions, the cumulative release was GDSS-PCD1 > GDSS-PCD2 > GDSS-PCD3 > GDSS-PCD4. Compared with the other three hydrogels, the GDSS-PCD1 hydrogels had the largest pore size and the loosest structure, which resulted in the fastest release rate. Therefore, the release rate of hydrogels was closely related to their structure. As the crosslinking degree of the hydrogel increased, the structure of the hydrogels became denser, and the release rate decreased.

The release rate and cumulative release of the same hydrogel decreased with increasing buffer pH. After release for 12 h, the cumulative release of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels in a buffer solution of pH 3 was 80.01 %, 74.63 %, 71.32 % and 66.04 %, respectively. The cumulative release rates of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels in a buffer solution of pH 9 were 33.45 %, 29.01 %, 27.34 % and 23.54 %, respectively. The release of S-PCDs was faster under acidic conditions (pH 3 and pH 5) and inhibited under alkaline conditions (pH 9) compared with neutral conditions (pH 7). The GDSS-PCD hydrogels had a good pH response to release S-PCDs and achieved the good release on demand.

To further understand the release mechanism, four mathematical models, namely, zero-order kinetics, first-order kinetics, Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models, were used to study the release of S-PCDs under different pH conditions. The release data of S-PCDs fitted to different models are listed in Table 2 . The best-fit model was determined by goodness-of-fit (R2). For the zero-order kinetic model, the R2 values obtained were all <0.9, indicating that the release of S-PCDs did not fit the zero-order kinetic model. Based on the R2 values fitted to the different models, the R2 values of first-order kinetics for all hydrogels were >0.95 at different pH values, indicating that the first-order kinetic release model was very satisfactory. The R2 of the Higuchi model was slightly lower, indicating that the degree of fitting was slightly worse than that of the first-order fitting model.

The diffusion constant (n) in the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was used to discuss the type of release mechanism. The n ≤ 0.43 for all GDSS-PCD hydrogels demonstrated that the release of S-PCDs from hydrogels was Fickian release. Therefore, the release of S-PCDs was primarily a diffusion process, mainly osmotic pressure-driven diffusion caused by the difference in concentration between the inside and outside of the hydrogel. Due to the water absorption and swelling of hydrogels, S-PCDs are transferred from the inside to the outside of the hydrogels by diffusion and swelling. Due to the degradation of the hydrogel, the S-PCDs crosslinked to the hydrogel through covalent bonds were gradually released with the loosening of the pore structure.

In summary, the relatively loose network structure and large pore size of different hydrogels promote the release of S-PCDs from hydrogels at the same pH. pH affects the stability of hydrogen bonds in hydrogels [35]. When the pH decreases, free hydrogen ions increase, and free hydrogen ions can cover negatively charged atoms on both sides, leading to the reduction and breakage of hydrogen bonds. Similarly, under extremely alkaline conditions, there are too many hydroxides and too few free hydrogen ions in the solution to form abundant hydrogen bonds. Therefore, too much acid or alkali will cause hydrogen bond breakage in the hydrogel, thus releasing CDs [36]. The –NH2 in the hydrogel also protonates in acidic solution, and the electrical repulsion makes the release faster. Furthermore, the accelerated release of S-PCDs from carbon dot hydrogels in acidic environments can also be attributed to the acid instability of Schiff bases [20]. Bacterial growth and metabolism typically produce an acidic environment [37], allowing for the release of S-PCDs in large quantities where there are more bacteria and the release of S-PCDs slowly where there are relatively few bacteria. This on-demand release hydrogel can be used to fight foodborne bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of carbon dot hydrogels
Analysis of antibacterial activity by the spread plate method
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of GDSS-PCD hydrogels was investigated by the spread plate method with a treated suspension of S. aureus. The total number of S. aureus colonies treated with GDS hydrogel did not change much compared with the control group (Fig. 8 b1). The lower inhibition rate of the GDS hydrogel was 8.37 %, which may be due to the porous structure of GDS hydrogel and the adsorption of surface-active groups to bacteria. S-PCDs significantly inhibited the growth of S. aureus, reduced the number of colonies, and significantly increased the antibacterial rate to 74.87 % (Fig. 8c1) (P < 0.05). Compared with the GDS hydrogel and S-PCDs, the GDSS-PCD hydrogels significantly inhibited bacterial growth and reduced the number of colonies (Fig. 8d1-g1). In addition, the results of antibacterial activity also showed that the inhibition rates of GDSS-PCD1 − GDSS-PCD4 hydrogels were significantly enhanced compared to GDS hydrogel and S-PCDs (P < 0.05), but the difference in inhibition rate among the four hydrogels was not significant, which were 87.09 %, 88.19 %, 89.84 % and 91.21 %, respectively (Fig. 8h). The results of the inhibitory zone diameter also showed that the GDS hydrogel did not have an obvious inhibitory zone, but the inhibitory zone diameter and the inhibitory rate of GDSS-PCD hydrogels gradually increased with increasing S-PCD concentration (Supporting information).

The cell structure of untreated and treated S. aureus was analyzed by SEM. The control group had a smooth surface with no obvious damage, indicating that the untreated S. aureus maintained its intact morphology (Fig. 8a2). S. aureus treated with the GDS hydrogel still showed clean membrane edges and good bacterial morphology compared to the control group (Fig. 8b2). After S-PCDs treatment, the bacterial morphology changed and obvious bacterial damage appeared, which was mainly manifested in the breakdown of bacteria and content leakage (Fig. 8c2). After treatment with GDSS-PCD hydrogels, the bacteria were seriously damaged, the bacterial cell membrane was irregular, and cell membrane defects appeared on the bacterial surface, which further led to serious leakage of cell contents, resulting in bacterial inactivation (Fig. 8d2-g2). These results indicated that GDSS-PCD hydrogels can cause physical or mechanical damage to the outer membrane of microorganisms, leading to overall physiological dysfunction of the bacteria and even leakage of cytoplasmic components, which eventually leads to bacterial death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adhesion effect of hydrogels on bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The GDS hydrogel did not contain antibacterial substances and had antibacterial capacity. Its low bacterial inhibition rate may be related to the porous structure of the hydrogel and the adsorption of the active groups on the surface of bacteria. In addition, the surface of S-PCDs contained a large number of C, N and O elements. After the introduction of S-PCDs into the GDS hydrogel, the adhesion of GDSS-PCD hydrogels to bacteria was enhanced, and the antibacterial activity was further increased due to the increase in active groups on their surfaces. Therefore, SEM was used to further analyze the adhesion of the GDS hydrogel and GDSS-PCD1 hydrogel to S. aureus. The results showed that there was a small amount of bacterial adhesion on the surface of the GDS hydrogel, the adhered bacterial cell membrane was smooth, and the bacterial structure was relatively complete (Fig. 8i). Compared with the GDS hydrogel, the number of bacteria on the surface of the GDSS-PCD1 hydrogel increased significantly, and some bacteria showed cell surface damage. This result proved that carbon dot hydrogels had a stronger adsorption capacity and damage ability to bacterial cell membranes, indicating their stronger antibacterial activity (Fig. 8j). Due to the synergistic action of GDS hydrogels and S-PCDs, carbon dot hydrogels had enhanced antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial mechanism of carbon dot hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, the synergistic antibacterial mechanism of S-PCDs and hydrogel was hypothesized. The S-PCDs on the surface of the hydrogel through hydrogen bonds were first released into the bacterial suspension to play an antibacterial role. The internal S-PCDs mainly played a role in two aspects, the carbon dot hydrogel had a porous structure, which can adsorb bacteria, and bacteria entering the hydrogel will interact with S-PCDs through electrostatic interactions [6]. Second, the acidic environment generated by the growth and metabolism of a large number of bacteria can destroy hydrogen bonds and dynamic imine bonds of the carbon dot hydrogels, which trigger the release of carbon dots for antibacterial purposes [20]. Hydrogels played a synergistic antibacterial role in trapping bacteria. The main antibacterial function of GDSS-PCD hydrogels was S-PCDs, and the antibacterial mechanism was closely related to S-PCDs. To put it simply, since the bacterial cell membrane is the first and most critical protective barrier of bacteria, small and highly positively charged S-PCDs contact the cell membrane through electrostatic interactions, and then penetrate the bacterial body, ultimately altering the permeability of the cell membrane and ultimately killing the bacteria [38]. In addition, the ability of S-PCDs to disrupt cell membranes is reflected in the leakage of bacterial contents, such as nucleic acids and proteins [38,39]. In another pathway, S-PCDs produce large amounts of ROS, and elevated ROS levels are known to damage intracellular DNA, proteins, and enzymes, thereby interfering with normal bacterial metabolism and killing bacteria.

In conclusion, carbon dot hydrogels (GDSS-PCD) were constructed by using GDS hydrogel as a supporting carrier carrying S-PCDs with strong antibacterial activity. A large number of active sites existed on the surface of gelatin, dialdehyde starch and S-PCDs; therefore, a three-dimensional hydrogel network was constructed by physical and chemical synergistic crosslinking. By adjusting the concentration of S-PCDs, the micropore size of hydrogels can be adjusted to improve the mechanical and rheological properties of hydrogels. The pH-responsive hydrogels can release S-PCDs on demand according to the environmental pH value. In addition, the GDSS-PCD hydrogels had both release and adsorption antibacterial effects. This on-demand release antibacterial hydrogel could be used in biomedical and safety applications.
